blocks.content: The town was busier now, but not crowded. He walked with no firm destination, which was how he usually found the best places. Planning too hard made his art stiff. Wandering gave it room to breathe.

He passed the bookstore, the bakery, the narrow alley painted with a faded mural from some festival years ago. He paused there, considering the texture of the bricks and the way ivy split the wall in uneven lines.
Then laughter carried from down the street.
That was ridiculous, of course. He had heard her laugh once. Maybe twice. He had no business recognizing it.
Cassandra crossed toward the park with her friend, still carrying the purple dinosaur. She moved through the town like she was collecting pieces of it.
Ethan stopped beside the alley wall.
Because something about her seemed impossible to sketch quickly enough.
Then, as if the world wanted to punish him for noticing, she turned.
For half a second, their eyes met.
Not a big smile. Not a romantic one. Just a quick, bright flash of acknowledgement sent across the street like a thrown match.
Ethan looked down immediately, pretending to search his bag.
Smooth. Very normal. Award-winning human behavior.
When he looked up again, she was walking away.
The sketchbook guy from the café window.
He was standing near the alley with a bag over one shoulder and a coffee in hand, looking like he had been caught doing something illegal by accident.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: That was not a no-one smile.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Absolutely. That was at least a someone-adjacent smile.
Cassandra glanced back once more, but the guy had started walking in the opposite direction, head down, shoulders slightly hunched as if apologizing to the sidewalk for taking up space.
Something about that tugged at her.
Some people hid behind volume. Some behind silence.
Cassandra knew a thing or two about hiding in plain sight.
